1: A type: “From inside the fish, Jonah prayed to the LORD his God.” Prayer from within the belly of the fish — prayer from what amounts to the grave — is the OT form of Christ's intercession from within death. Hebrews 5:7 says Christ “offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard.” Jonah in the fish praying and being heard (v10: YHWH commanded the fish) is the type of Christ in death praying and being heard in the resurrection. The prayer that rises from the deepest place is heard — this is the theological principle that Jonah 2 demonstrates and the resurrection confirms.
2: A type: “From the depths of Sheol I cried for help, and you heard my voice.” Jonah describes his location as Sheol — the realm of the dead. His prayer from Sheol is the OT type of Christ's descent to the realm of death and his being heard there. Peter at Pentecost cites Psalms 16:10 (“you will not abandon my soul to Hades, or let your Holy One see corruption”) as fulfilled in Christ's resurrection (Acts 2:27-31) — the same register of dying and being heard that Jonah's prayer inhabits. YHWH hears prayer from the depths of death: this is what the resurrection demonstrates.
3: A shadow: “All your waves and breakers swept over me.” The waves as the expression of YHWH's wrath against the guilty Jonah — the same language appears in Psalms 88:7 (“your wrath lies heavy upon me, and you overwhelm me with all your waves”) and Psalms 42:7 (“all your waves and your billows have gone over me”, cited in the passion context). Christ on the cross undergoes the full force of divine wrath — the waves of YHWH's judgment that Jonah experienced because of his own sin, Christ endured on behalf of ours (Isaiah 53:6: “the LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all”).
4: A shadow: “I have been banished from your sight.” The experience of spiritual abandonment — separation from the presence of YHWH — is among the most devastating things in Jonah's prayer. Christ's cry from the cross — “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” (Matthew 27:46, citing Psalms 22:1) — is the ultimate, and most undeserved, form of this abandonment. Where Jonah is banished for his sin, Christ is forsaken for ours. The “banishment from YHWH's sight” that Jonah experiences as consequence, Christ endures as substitution: he is forsaken so that we need not be.
5: A shadow: “The engulfing waters threatened me, the deep surrounded me; seaweed was wrapped around my head.” The imagery of total immersion in the chaos-waters of death is the most visceral description of near-annihilation in Scripture. The passion narratives draw on the same Psalm tradition (Psalms 69:1-2: “the waters have come up to my neck... I have come into deep waters”, cited in John 15:25 and Acts 1:20 in passion contexts). Christ's passion is the fullest possible immersion in human death and abandonment — not just symbolic drowning but actual death — so that those who trust him are pulled from the waters.
6: A type: “You brought my life up from the pit, O LORD my God.” The verb alah (brought up) from the shachat (pit, grave) is the resurrection language of the Psalms. Psalms 16:10 (“you will not abandon my soul to the pit”) is cited by Peter (Acts 2:27) and Paul (Acts 13:35) as the OT text that anticipates Christ's resurrection. Jonah's emergence from the fish-belly is the enacted version of this psalm-prayer — being brought up from the pit. The type is explicit: the fish releases Jonah from death, as the tomb releases Christ on the third day, in both cases at YHWH's command.
7: A shadow: “When my life was ebbing away, I remembered you, LORD, and my prayer rose to you.” In extremis — “when my life was ebbing away” — Jonah turns to YHWH. This is the pattern of faith in the abyss. Christ's final words from the cross follow this pattern: “Father, into your hands I commit my spirit” (Luke 23:46, citing Psalms 31:5) is the ultimate “remembering YHWH” in the moment of death. Where Jonah's life ebbs because of his own flight, Christ's life ebbs by voluntary self-giving. Both prayers rise from the deepest place; both are heard; both result in life from death.
8: A shadow: “Those who cling to worthless idols forfeit the grace that could be theirs.” This brief doctrinal statement within Jonah's prayer identifies the alternative to YHWH: idols that cannot respond, save, or offer mercy (chesed, steadfast love). Paul develops the same contrast in Romans: those who exchanged the truth of God for a lie (idols) are separated from the grace available in the one true God (Romans 1:21-25); but in Christ, grace is freely available to all who abandon idols and turn to the living God (1 Thessalonians 1:9-10). Jonah preaches to idol-worshipping Nineveh; the gospel calls all idol-worshippers to the grace Jonah here confesses.
9: A direct anticipation: “Salvation is from the LORD” (yeshuah lADONAI). Jonah's confession from the fish's belly is the theological summary of the entire book — and the name of Jesus. The Hebrew yeshuah (salvation, deliverance) is the root of the name Yeshua (Jesus), which the angel explains as “he will save his people from their sins” (Matthew 1:21). What Jonah confesses abstractly from within death — salvation belongs to YHWH alone, not to human effort or Gentile gods — Christ embodies concretely: he is the salvation that belongs to YHWH, made flesh and dwelling among us. The confession rises from the depths; the Savior descends into the depths to make the confession true.
10: A type: “The LORD spoke to the fish, and it vomited Jonah onto dry land.” At YHWH's word, the fish releases its captive; Jonah emerges from death to new life and mission. This is the enacted version of the type Jesus names in Matthew 12:40: just as Jonah came out of the fish on the third day, so the Son of Man will be raised from the heart of the earth. YHWH's authority over the fish — death cannot hold its captive beyond the appointed time — is the same authority that raises Christ: “God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it” (Acts 2:24). Death cannot hold the one YHWH releases.
